retrieved_docs.title: Stargardt Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: ix) Microperimetry: The ProgStar study is a reliable investigation for detecting macular function in studies evaluating the various treatment modalities. The study results showed that measuring visual acuity is not a sensitive outcome for monitoring the disease in various treatment trials. VF testing can miss small scotomas. Patients with STGD1 show varying degrees of reduced sensitivity on microperimetry (MP), depending on the stage of the disease. Macular sensitivity is reduced in the areas with flecks and abnormal AF.

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Visual Acuity -- Technique or Treatment -- Methods of Assessing Visual Acuity
retrieved_docs.content: The chart size is 80 cm (height) x 70 cm (width). Chart appearance is V-like when viewed from a distance. Disadvantages of the chart include longer examination times and larger chart sizes. The most commonly used LogMAR charts are the Bailey-Lovie and ETDRS charts (see Image. ETDRS Chart).

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Visual Acuity -- Technique or Treatment -- Methods of Assessing Visual Acuity
retrieved_docs.content: ETDRS chart: The ETDRS chart was described by Ferris and colleagues at the National Eye Institute, United States. [23] The ETDRS chart uses a luminance of 160 cd/m 2 . [19] Each optotype (Sloan letter, see below) has the same height and width; the ratio is 5:5. Thus, these letters are wider compared to the letters of the Bailey-Lovie chart. The ETDRS chart was designed to be read at a distance of 4 meters instead of 6 meters. [23] The ETDRS chart may, therefore, be used to check visual acuity in smaller examination rooms.

retrieved_docs.title: Stargardt Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The ProgStar study results also found the mean sensitivity reduces by around 0.68 dB yearly. [55] The "Scotopic Microperimetric Assessment of Rod function in Stargardt Disease" (SMART) study, the ancillary study to ProgStart, demonstrated that the loss of scotopic macular function in the extrafoveal spots was faster than the loss of mesopic macular function. [56] x) Visual electrophysiology: Lois and colleagues classified STGD1 cases into 3 groups based on their electrophysiological properties. [57] Group 1 shows severe abnormalities in pattern electroretinogram (PERG) with normal photopic and scotopic full-field electroretinogram (ffERG). Group 2 includes additional abnormalities (loss) in photopic ffERG (abnormal cone function). Group 3 shows loss of both photopic and scotopic full-field ERG response (abnormal cone and rod function). [57] [58] [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Snellen Chart -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: ETDRS : Developed in 1982 by a researcher named Rick Ferris, the ETDRS is a modification of the Bailey-Lovie chart to overcome the limitations of the Snellen chart. The ETDRS chart is tested from a shorter distance (13 feet rather than 20 feet), has the same amount of letters in every row (five letters each), and has an equal spacing of both the letters and the rows on a logarithmic scale. Shamir et al. found the ETDRS to be significantly more accurate and reproducible than the Snellen chart, yet its adoption in clinical practice has been limited due to its cost, longer testing time, and size of the chart. [3] Jäger : Invented in 1854 by the Austrian ophthalmologist Jäger, this chart tests near vision. The card contains 11 short paragraphs, which are numbered from 1 (smallest font) to 11 (largest font). The card is held roughly 14 inches from the nose at a comfortable reading distance for testing.

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Visual Acuity -- Technique or Treatment -- Methods of Assessing Visual Acuity
retrieved_docs.content: The Snellen fraction at 4 meters may be converted to conventional American notation by multiplying the numerator and the denominator by 5. Thus, 4/5 is equivalent to 20/25. At 4 meters, the visual acuity is maximum, and the dispersion of visual acuity is minimum. [24] Refraction for infinity can be calculated by deducting 0.25 diopter from the refraction at 4 meters. Nonstandard distances used with the ETDRS chart are taken in a geometrical progression (3.2 m, 2.5 m, 2 m, 1.6 m). The height and width of the chart are 60.3 cm and 63.5 cm, respectively. The reading of a single letter is allowed once only.

retrieved_docs.title: Macular Degeneration -- Treatment / Management -- Antiangiogenic Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: It binds both VEGF and placental growth factor and has good retinal penetration. VIEW 1 and 2 studies have documented that aflibercept is non-inferior to ranibizumab in terms of gain in the number of ETDRS letters while measuring visual acuity at the end of the treatment period. A higher dose of aflibercept administered every two months instead of monthly was also seen to be as effective as a monthly dose of ranibizumab. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Snellen Chart -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: While the Snellen chart is prized as a quick, low cost, and portable tool, there are several drawbacks to its use. These disadvantages, outlined below, gave birth to the improvements seen in newer charts, such as the ETDRS.

retrieved_docs.title: Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy -- Evaluation -- Clinical Examination
retrieved_docs.content: Visual acuity testing: This testing helps establish the baseline and monitor progression or recovery. The visual acuity typically  ranges from 20/20 to counting fingers, depending on the extent of optic nerve involvement. Visual acuity may improve over weeks in about 30% of patients. [4] Pupillary eeflex evaluation: RAPD is typically present in unilateral or asymmetric disease. In bilateral cases, the defect may be masked, and careful comparison is needed. [65] Colour vision testing: This testing is performed using Ishihara plates or the Farnsworth D-15 test. Dyschromatopsia is common but less pronounced than in optic neuritis. [66] Fundus examination: Optic disc edema (segmental or diffuse) is evident in the acute phase of the disease. Flame-shaped hemorrhages may be present. The fellow eye may show a small cup-to-disc ratio (disc-at-risk). [28] Visual field assessment:

retrieved_docs.title: Stargardt Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: viii) Visual field: [53] This test shows a progressive central scotoma. Loss of mid and peripheral VFs is usually associated with more severe disease. Schroeder et al  classified the VF severity into 3 groups. Group 1 has a central scotoma within 10°, group 2 has central scotomas within 10° to 35°, and group 3 only has "temporal residues." [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Macular Edema -- Treatment / Management -- Ocular Laser
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetic macular edema: Laser therapy is recommended for patients with inadequate response to anti-VEGF therapy. Healthcare providers typically utilize double diode or YAG photocoagulation lasers to treat diabetic macular edema. The ETDRS reveals that laser reduces the risk of moderate visual loss, or a loss of 15 letters on the EDTRS visual chart or a doubling of the visual angle, at 36 months. After 6 weeks, clinicians can treat any untreated initial lesions. Repeat treatment should be scheduled at least 4 months after the initial session if no initial lesions remain. [99] Diabetic macular edema treatment techniques: The various techniques to manage diabetic macular edema are listed below.

retrieved_docs.title: Stargardt Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: i) Color fundus photography ( Figure 1. ) is helpful in the documentation and monitoring of the type of retinal changes and extent of the disease. The Fishman classification is based on color fundus photographs. [35] Stage 1 is characterized by macular pigmentary changes, which may range from faint or irregular pigment mottling to bronze-beaten appearance; a ring of pisciform flecks within 1 disc-diameter (DD) on all sides of the fovea; and a normal electroretinogram (ERG) and electrooculogram (EOG). The presence of pisciform flecks beyond 1 DD of the fovea and may extend beyond the vascular arcades and nasal to the optic nerve; a normal ERG and EOG except for occasional subnormal cone/rod response; and prolonged dark adaptation is representative of stage 2. Atrophic macular changes replacing the previously recognized flecks, subnormal EOG and ERG responses, and central and mid-peripheral and peripheral visual field defects characterize stage 3. Stage 4 occurs with extensive atrophy throughout the entire fundus, reduced ERG responses, and moderate to extensive peripheral field restriction. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitreous Hemorrhage -- Treatment / Management -- Photocoagulation
retrieved_docs.content: The Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) suggested that in older patients with Type 2 DM with severe NPDR or early PDR, a prompt laser was better than deferral when compared to young patients with type 1 diabetes. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ) is clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). Both conditions represent severe microvascular complications of long‑standing diabetes mellitus, and the presence of diabetic retinopathy often signals an increased risk of progression to advanced kidney failure. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians, as early detection and management of retinal changes can prompt more aggressive monitoring of renal function, and coordinated care can help mitigate the morbidity associated with simultaneous vision loss and the need for renal replacement therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين) and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Peripheral involvement includes peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis and peripheral focal retinitis with retinochoroiditis. Across all entities, the choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical locus of the characteristic lesions, underscoring its central role in pathophysiology, clinical assessment, precise diagnosis, and tailored management of choroidal and retinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1709
retrieved_docs.content: The condition تمزق الشبكية (retinal tear) is directly linked to the need for repair of retinal tear; detachment, indicating that a retinal tear often requires surgical repair to prevent or treat detachment. This relationship highlights the importance of timely intervention for retinal tears to preserve visual function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2169
retrieved_docs.content: Acute disorder of hemodialysis, sudden acquired retinal degeneration, and متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى (dialysis dysfunction syndrome) are all described as having a clinical course characterized by sudden onset. The “sudden onset” qualifier indicates that the manifestations of these conditions develop rapidly, which is a key feature for clinicians to recognize when diagnosing and managing these disorders. Understanding that a rapid, abrupt presentation is common to these entities helps guide timely evaluation and appropriate therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: The retina serves as the primary anatomic site for a variety of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and retinopathy. In patients with secondary diabetes mellitus, both non‑proliferative retinopathy and moderate non‑proliferative retinopathy are identified as retinal findings, underscoring that diabetic retinopathy manifests within the retinal tissue. Additionally, the condition referred to as داء ريفسام also presents its findings in the retina. Recognizing the retina as the common location for these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management in ophthalmic and systemic disease care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1253
retrieved_docs.content: حصوي (calcific degeneration of choroidal or retinal tissue), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal atrophy) and حثل المشيمية حول الحُليلة، جزئي (partial sclerosis of the choroid surrounding the macula). Together, these entities illustrate the extensive range of inflammatory, vascular, hemorrhagic, degenerative, and structural pathologies classified under Disorders of choroid and retina, underscoring the need for precise diagnosis and targeted management to preserve visual function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2476
retrieved_docs.content: The retina is the anatomical site where a variety of ocular pathologies are observed, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusion, and retinopathy. Both moderate nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus and non‑proliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus are identified in the retina, highlighting its role as the primary finding site for diabetic‑related retinal disease. In addition, the condition referred to in Arabic as داء ريفسام also manifests in the retina. Recognizing the retina as the common location for these diverse findings supports focused clinical assessment and appropriate management of retinal disorders.

Additional specific sub‑conditions listed under the same category are انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي, تنكس حصوي, and حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي. Together, these entities illustrate the extensive and heterogeneous nature of choroidal and retinal disorders, underscoring the importance of precise classification for effective patient care, targeted research, and accurate medical coding.

Together, these entities illustrate diverse etiologies—vascular, inflammatory, traumatic, postoperative, degenerative, and genetic—that affect the choroid and retina, underscoring the importance of precise classification for diagnosis, management, and prognostication in ophthalmic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus—all localized to the retina. The retina is also the primary site for retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and various other retinopathies. Therapeutic interventions targeting these lesions comprise destructive procedures on the posterior segment of the eye, categorized under “destruction of lesion of retina and choroid,” with radiation‑based destruction of localized retinal lesions (e.g., macular edema or tumors) as a further sub‑category. The disease داء ريفسام is likewise identified with retinal findings, underscoring the eye’s role in its manifestation. Clinical assessment employs the concept of “general clinical state” to interpret both biologic function failure and uncontrolled disease conditions; uncontrolled disease conditions are associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, highlighting the need for vigilant monitoring. Biologic function failure is linked to hepatic‑renal failure, indicating a critical organ‑system

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279
retrieved_docs.content: The retina is the anatomical site where a range of ocular pathologies are identified, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusion, and retinopathy. Both Non‑proliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus and Retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus are specifically noted to have their findings in the retina. The Arabic‑language term داء ريفسام, which denotes retinal disease, is likewise localized to the retina. Recognizing that these diverse conditions share the retina as their finding site supports focused clinical assessment and management of retinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2624
retrieved_docs.content: The retina serves as the anatomical site for a range of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusion, and retinopathy. Both moderate nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus and retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus are identified within the retina, indicating that diabetic retinal disease manifests at this location. Additionally, the condition referred to in Arabic as داء ريفسام also has its findings localized to the retina. Recognizing the retina as the common finding site for these diverse lesions aids clinicians in targeted examination and management of retinal disorders.
